skill.name: markdown-pro
skill.description: Create polished Markdown documentation for projects, including README files, changelogs, contribution guides, tables of contents, badges, and formatting guidance.
skill.summary: Create professional Markdown documentation, templates, and helper-generated project docs.
skill.author: AutumnsGrove

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown examples, badges, placeholder API keys, and fenced shell commands. The real executable surface is scripts/markdown_helper.py, which runs git log and can write a changelog file. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, malicious network calls, or confirmed blocker behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: Git Metadata Collection Through Subprocess
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE with legitimate purpose: the helper runs git log through subprocess.run and captures commit metadata. The command uses a fixed argument list and shell=False by default, so command injection is not evident. It can still expose commit subjects, author names, and author emails in generated changelogs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call and parsed commit fields are directly visible in the helper script. Risk is moderated because arguments are structured and no shell execution is used.

low_findings.title: User-Selected File Write for Changelog Output
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE with expected behavior: the changelog command writes generated text to the path provided by the user. This is normal for a documentation helper, but users should avoid passing sensitive or unintended paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write is explicit and easy to confirm. The behavior is bounded to generated changelog content and requires a user-provided output option.

low_findings.title: Static Command Alerts Are Mostly Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for most external command alerts: the detected commands are inside Markdown templates and usage examples. They document common project workflows and are not automatically executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced Markdown or template content, not executable program flow. The separate helper script contains the only confirmed subprocess use.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Credentials and URLs in Documentation
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for env_access and network alerts: the API keys and URLs are placeholders in documentation examples. No code path reads environment secrets or sends data to a network endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use placeholder strings such as api_key values and badge URLs. No evidence found of process environment access or outbound network requests in executable code.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Alerts Match Documentation Text
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: weak cryptographic algorithm alerts appear to match ordinary Markdown or documentation terms. No evidence found of cryptographic hashing, encryption, password storage, or security-sensitive crypto code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are prose or changelog text rather than crypto implementation. The scanner produced many blocker labels, but semantic review found no crypto operations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess Invocation of Git
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper invokes git log with subprocess.run to build changelog entries. This is legitimate for the skill, but it should be treated as local command execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct subprocess.run usage is present, but it uses a structured argument list and no shell=True setting.

dangerous_patterns.title: Generated Documentation File Write
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper can write generated changelog content to an output file. This is expected functionality, but it modifies the local filesystem.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The open call with write mode is explicit and tied to the changelog output option.

content.value_statement: Project documentation is often incomplete, inconsistent, or hard to maintain. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code produce structured Markdown documents with reusable templates.
actual_capabilities: Drafts README structures with badges, installation steps, usage examples, and license sections.
Creates changelog formats based on Keep a Changelog and semantic versioning conventions.
Provides contribution guide templates with issue, branch, commit, and pull request workflows.
Generates table of contents text from Markdown headings through the helper script.
Validates local Markdown links through the helper script and reports broken references.
Formats API documentation examples with parameters, return values, errors, and examples.
limitations: The helper script only validates local links and skips external URL checks.
Changelog generation depends on local git history and commit message quality.
Templates use placeholder project names, URLs, and API examples that require review.
The skill does not publish documentation or manage release workflows by itself.

use_cases.title: Launch a New Open Source Project
use_cases.description: Create a complete README and contribution guide before public release.

use_cases.title: Standardize Internal Developer Docs
use_cases.description: Apply consistent Markdown structure across service READMEs and API references.

use_cases.title: Prepare Release Notes
use_cases.description: Turn git history and conventional commits into organized changelog sections.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic README
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a README for this project with overview, installation, usage, configuration, contributing, and license sections.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Existing Documentation
prompt_templates.scenario: Rewrite and structure an existing document
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Markdown file and improve structure, headings, examples, links, and readability while preserving technical meaning.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Contribution Guide
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a CONTRIBUTING guide for this repository with setup steps, branch naming, testing, commits, pull requests, and review expectations.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Release Documentation
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a changelog and release notes from these commits. Group changes by type and call out breaking changes clearly.

output_examples.input: A small Python package needs its first README.
output_examples.output: A structured README with project overview, badges, installation commands, usage flow, API notes, contribution steps, and license text.

output_examples.input: A repository has several releases but no changelog.
output_examples.output: A chronological changelog with Added, Changed, Fixed, Security, and Breaking Changes sections.

output_examples.input: A team wants consistent contributor instructions.
output_examples.output: A contribution guide covering setup, issue selection, branch workflow, test expectations, pull requests, and conduct standards.

best_practices: Review generated examples and replace every placeholder before publishing documentation.
Run helper scripts only inside repositories you trust and understand.
Keep README, changelog, and contribution guide updates part of each release process.
anti_patterns: Do not publish template URLs, package names, or API keys without replacing them.
Do not generate changelogs from noisy commits without editorial review.
Do not use the helper script output path for files you do not intend to replace.

faq.question: Can this skill create a complete README?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides sections for overview, installation, usage, API reference, contributing, and license details.

faq.question: Does it require external services?
faq.answer: No. The documented helper script works locally and does not make network requests.

faq.question: Can it generate a changelog from git history?
faq.answer: Yes. The helper script reads local git commits and groups entries into changelog categories.

faq.question: Does it validate external links?
faq.answer: No. The helper skips HTTP, HTTPS, mail, and anchor links during validation.

faq.question: Is this safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: Use care. Changelog generation can include commit subjects, author names, and author emails.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
